query: X drafts sync across devices mobile web rollout recent update

mako.raw:
{"query":"X drafts sync across devices mobile web rollout recent update","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1525,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://typefully.com/blog/sync-x-twitter-drafts-web-mobile","title":"How to Sync X (Twitter) Drafts Between Web and Mobile","content":"After many years of requests, X (Twitter) has finally rolled out synced drafts between mobile and web.
This means drafts you start on the X mobile app will now be there when you log in on the web version—and vice versa.
If you've been frustrated by losing half-written posts when switching devices, this update is a game-changer.
Threads with multiple images, videos, or GIFs don't always sync reliably.
There's no way to see which times get the most engagement or to queue posts across multiple accounts easily.
Typefully lets you post the same content (or customize it) across X, LinkedIn, Threads, and Bluesky with a single click."},{"url":"https://technext24.com/2025/10/14/x-launch-synced-drafts-feature-app-web/","title":"X rolls out 'synced drafts' feature that allows users to continue draft made via app on web","content":"Social Media platform X (formerly Twitter) is rolling out synced drafts between its mobile app and web platform.
“After 10 years of asking, we are finally rolling out synced drafts: Drafts written on the X app will now be there when you log in on the web,” he said.
Bier explained that the Android app is currently undergoing a rebuilding process, and the synced draft feature will roll out with the next app update.
A recent report in 2025 shows that X possesses 650 million monthly active users (MAU) and 240 to 300 million daily active users (DAU)."},{"url":"https://mashable.com/article/twitter-web-app-saved-drafts-scheduled-tweets?utm_cid=a-seealso","title":"Twitter's web app now supports saved drafts and scheduled tweets | Mashable","content":"As of now, tweet drafts saved on desktop or mobile web are accessible via desktop or mobile web only; unfinished thoughts do not currently sync across platforms."},{"url":"https://tech-ish.com/2025/10/14/x-finally-rolls-out-synced-drafts-across-web-and-app/","title":"X finally rolls out synced drafts across web and app - tech-ish","content":"This update allows users to start composing a draft on their mobile device and seamlessly continue editing it on the web, or vice versa.
Nikita Bier, X’s Head of Product, announced the feature on the platform, acknowledging the decade-long wait for this functionality.
Previously, drafts created on one device were not accessible on another, leading to a fragmented user experience."},{"url":"https://eastleighvoice.co.ke/technology/225909/x-lets-you-start-a-post-on-your-iphone-and-finish-on-desktop-with-new-draft-sync-feature","title":"X lets you start a post on your iPhone and finish on desktop with new draft sync feature | The Eastleigh Voice","content":"The draft syncing update, now available on iOS and the web, allows an unfinished post written on an iPhone to be accessed instantly from a desktop browser, with no need to copy and paste text or email yourself ideas.
X has rolled out a new feature that lets users start writing a post on one device and finish it on another, bridging a gap that has frustrated creators and casual users alike.
“We’ve made it seamless to move between your phone and your computer, drafts now follow you automatically,” confirmed Nikita Bier, X’s Head of Product, in a post announcing the change.
Currently, the feature supports text-only drafts, meaning attachments such as photos, GIFs, or videos will not yet sync between devices.
This global rollout strategy aligns with X’s recent approach to feature deployment, which prioritises major markets simultaneously rather than region by region.

Forbes global rich list (2026)"}]}

The first tweets from the public appeared on July 15, 2006, several months after a soft launch. A man’s hands as he prepares to type a tweet on his mobile phone. At the height of its influence, the platform — renamed X in 2023 — gave the powerful and the unsung alike a place to preen and pontificate, 140 characters (later 280 characters) at a time. Its memes and viral videos distracted and entertained, but Twitter also became the preferred way for governors and heads of state — and one American president in particular — to communicate with the public. On July 15, 2006, a new era of provocative hot takes, kneejerk reactions and random thoughts on social media ushered itself in with the public debut of Twitter.
